coredata.dc:title: Histidine-derived carbon dots for redox modulation and gut microbiota regulation in inflammatory bowel disease therapy

coredata.dc:description: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a chronic inflammation of the colon that is becoming increasingly prevalent. As a result, there is a growing demand for safe, effective, and non-addictive drugs. Herein, we are inspired by using carbon dots-based nanomedicines to regulate redox balance and alleviate inflammatory diseases. Specifically, histidine-derived carbon dots (His-CDs) with antioxidant and multi-enzyme (superoxide dismutase, catalase) activities are synthesized, which not only improve intestinal redox balance, but also demonstrate promising potential in modulating gut microbiota for the treatment of IBD. To understand that underlying activities mechanisms of His-CDs, their precursors, structures, and band gaps are carefully analyzed. Our investigations show that His-CDs effectively reduce oxidative damage to cells and nematodes by scavenging free radicals. Furthermore, we confirm the anti-IBD effects of His-CDs at both cellular and mouse levels. Importantly, our research reveal that His-CDs restore intestinal homeostasis in colitis by regulating the gut microbiota. We further validate this finding through fecal microbiota transplantation, which demonstrate an increase in the abundance of beneficial bacteria in the intestinal tract following the administration of His-CDs. This investigation not only expands the knowledge of nanozymes, but also offers a promising nanomedicine approach for IBD therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of His-CDs. (a) TEM images of His-CDs. Scale bar: 10 nm. The inset is HRTEM image of His-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution histogram and (c) XRD pattern of His-CDs. (d) Survey XPS spectrum and High-resolution (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s spectra of His-CDs.

His-CDs with free radical scavenging activities protected cells/nematodes from oxidative damage. (a) DPPH and (b) ABTS free radical scavenging activity of His-CDs and two precursors at different concentrations, illustrated as solution photos for each group. (c) SOD-like enzyme inhibitior rate of His-CDs and two precursors at different concentrations. (d) Apoptosis of NCM460 cells in different treatment groups under oxidative stress model of H2O2. (e) DCFDA detection photos and (f) relative intensity of ROS in nematodes under different treatment conditions. (g) MDA content in different treatment groups. (h) mRNA levels of SOD-1 and (i) GST-1 in different treatment groups. Data were obtained from three separate experiments with n = 3 in each group. Data are shown as mean ± standard deviation. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

His-CDs relieved DSS-induced acute colitis. (a) Flow chart of the mouse experimental protocol. (b) Weight changes and overall weight loss of mice in each group from days 7 to 21. (c) Disease Activity Index (DAI) scores of each group of mice on days 7 to 21. (d) Colon length statistics. (e) Representative colonic photographs of each group of mice. (f) Histopathological scores of hematoxylin eosin staining in colon sections of mice in each group. (g-j) Redox related indicators: SOD (g), GSH (h), MDA (i), and MPO (j) contents in the colon tissues from healthy and colitis mice. (k-n) Gene expression: Relative transcript level of pro-inflammatory (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-18) cytokines and anti-inflammatory (IL-10) in colon tissues, as determined by RT-qPCR. Data were obtained from three separate experiments with n = 6 mice in each group. Data are shown as mean ± standard deviation. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

(a) Immunohistochemical photographs of colon tissue ZO-1 of mice in each group, with a scale of 100 μm and (b) average optical density determination. (c) Alcian blue staining photos of colon tissue of mice in each group, with a scale of 100 μm and (d) goblet cell count statistics. Relative expression of (e) occludin (f) Claudin-1 and (g) ZO-1 mRNA in colon tissue of mice in each group. (h) Protein expressions of tight junction protein occludin by the typical western blot. Data were obtained from three separate experiments with n = 3 mice in each group. Data are shown as mean ± standard deviation. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

His-CDs mitigated the changes in intestinal microbiota composition induced by sodium dextran sulfate (DSS). (a) The Venn diagram shows overlapping operational taxonomic units in the gut microbiota of the three groups of mice. (b) Assessment of β diversity based on principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). (c-e) Studies of α diversity based on simpson, sobs and ace indices. (f) The average relative abundance of taxa at the phylum and (g) genus level. (h-j) Abundance of norank_f_Muribaculaceae, Dubosiella, and Turicibacter in the intestinal microbiome levels of mice in each group. Data were obtained from three separate experiments with n = 6 mice in each group. Data were shown as mean ± standard deviation. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

Fecal microbiota transplantation can alleviate DSS induced colitis in mice. (a) Schematic diagram of fecal microbiota transplantation experiment. (b) Weight changes and overall weight loss of mice in each group from days 1 to 14. (c) Disease Activity Index (DAI) scores of mice in each group from days 1 to 14. (d) Representative colonic photographs of mice in each group and (e) colonic length statistics. (f) H&E staining photos of colon sections of mice in each group, with a scale of 100 μm. (g) The content of IL-1β protein in colon tissue of mice in each group. (h-j) Relative expression value of pro-inflammatory (IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-18) cytokines in colon tissues, as determined by RT-qPCR. Data were obtained from three separate experiments with n = 6 mice in each group. Data are shown as mean ± standard deviation. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

Synthesis of His-CDs and its mechanism of alleviating inflammatory bowel disease.

head.ce:title: Histidine-derived carbon dots for redox modulation and gut microbiota regulation in inflammatory bowel disease therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a chronic inflammation of the colon that is becoming increasingly prevalent. As a result, there is a growing demand for safe, effective, and non-addictive drugs. Herein, we are inspired by using carbon dots-based nanomedicines to regulate redox balance and alleviate inflammatory diseases. Specifically, histidine-derived carbon dots (His-CDs) with antioxidant and multi-enzyme (superoxide dismutase, catalase) activities are synthesized, which not only improve intestinal redox balance, but also demonstrate promising potential in modulating gut microbiota for the treatment of IBD. To understand that underlying activities mechanisms of His-CDs, their precursors, structures, and band gaps are carefully analyzed. Our investigations show that His-CDs effectively reduce oxidative damage to cells and nematodes by scavenging free radicals. Furthermore, we confirm the anti-IBD effects of His-CDs at both cellular and mouse levels. Importantly, our research reveal that His-CDs restore intestinal homeostasis in colitis by regulating the gut microbiota. We further validate this finding through fecal microbiota transplantation, which demonstrate an increase in the abundance of beneficial bacteria in the intestinal tract following the administration of His-CDs. This investigation not only expands the knowledge of nanozymes, but also offers a promising nanomedicine approach for IBD therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), encompassing ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, is a chronic, complex, and idiopathic inflammatory disorder that significantly impacts the quality of life for patients and has a substantial global morbidity burden [1,2]. The disruption of intestinal homeostasis is a characteristic and pathogenic feature of IBD, primarily resulting in alterations within the intestinal microenvironment (specifically elevated levels of reactive oxygen species [ROS]), dysbiosis of the gut microbiome, immune dysregulation, epithelial barrier and mucosal damage, as well as intestinal fibrosis [3,4]. While traditional therapeutics for IBD typically involve the administration of anti-inflammatory molecules (e.g., mesalazine) and immunosuppressive drugs (e.g., corticosteroids and anti-tumor necrosis factor antibodies) following surgery, these medicines often target only single factors associated with IBD and fail to achieve optimal clinical efficacy [5,6]. Moreover, their limited therapeutic targeting, low rates of remission, long-term toxicity, and steroid dependency restrict their clinical utility [7]. Consequently, there is an urgent demand for multifaceted intervention drugs with minimal side effects capable of restoring intestinal homeostasis.

Nanozymes, nanoparticles with enzymatic activity, have gained considerable attention in the biomedical field due to their high stability, cost-effectiveness, and ease of modification [8–10]. Within the realm of inflammation therapy, various nanozymes have shown significant potential [11–15]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel carbon based nanozyme, have shown great potential in biological imaging, biosensing, and therapy by combining physical and chemical properties such as fluorescence and antioxidant [16–19]. Compared with other carbon materials, CDs have superior optical and antioxidant properties, while CDs have better biocompatibility and easier surface functionalization compared with metal based nanozymes, making them a feasible strategy for treating diseases caused by oxidative stress. For inatance, the BWE-CDs extracted by Wang et al. from the water extract of broccoli exhibit excellent antioxidant properties, effectively clearing reactive oxygen species (ROS) from cells and zebrafish, and alleviating zebrafish inflammation [20]. They further utilized chemical reactions to modify the surface functional groups of BWE-CDs and found that their antioxidant mechanism originated from the C=C, –COOH, and –NH2 in the structure. Zhang's team has developed a CDs with superoxide dismutase (SOD) like activity, which can eliminate excessive ROS, protect cells from oxidative stress damage, reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and alleviate colitis [21]. The activated carbon derived carbon dots (C-dots) reported by Yan's team have SOD like enzyme activity and can be used to treat oxidative stress-related diseases [22]. More importantly, they revealed the structure and enzyme activity relationship of C-dots through chemical surface modification and theoretical calculations, demonstrating that its SOD activity depends on –OH and –COOH binding to O2•−, as well as −C=O conjugated to the π system for electron transfer. In addition, CDs derived from biomass or bioactive substances, such as broccoli [20], genistein [23], and chitosan [24], have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in eliminating excessive free radicals both internally and externally. However, the complex composition of biomass (e.g., proteins and organic acid) presents challenges in fully understanding the relationship between the structure and nanozyme activity of CDs. The difference between the effects of different batches also greatly limits the further application of biomass-derived CDs. As the basic components of proteins, amino acids play an indispensable role in maintaining nitrogen balance and normal physiological functions, and are ideal nitrogen sources for the synthesis of CDs. Citric acid, as a natural organic acid, is also the most commonly used carbon source in the preparation of CDs. Amino acid-derived CDs have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in eliminating excessive free radicals both internally and externally. For example, Liu et al. successfully fabricated Se-CDs using selenocystine as a precursor, enabling immediate scavenging of harmful ROS when internalized in cells [25]. Although CDs with rich surface functional groups are recognized for their various anti-inflammatory mechanisms such as clearing free radicals and regulating immune responses, the potential role of regulating gut microbiota to combat inflammation remains largely unexplored and underestimated.

The gut microbiota, a critical component of the intestinal microenvironment, plays a crucial role in maintaining intestinal homeostasis [26]. Imbalances in the intestinal flora can disrupt the metabolic functions of carbohydrates, lipids, and amino acids, leading to intestinal damage [27]. Through fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT), the microbial balance within a patient's gut can be reestablished, leading to improved gut function and potentially mitigating or even reversing intestinal damage. In recent years, there has been an increasing recognition that nanomaterials can effectively modulate the composition and structure of the gut microbiota to improve diseases [28–30]. For example, green carbon dots derived from Atractylodes Macrocephala have demonstrated significant biological effects in maintaining intestinal flora homeostasis in alcoholic gastric ulcer models [31]. Carbon dots derived from Coptidis Rhizoma (SCR-CDs) can enhance the diversity of the gut microbiota and restore the balance between beneficial and pathogenic bacteria [32]. The increased presence of probiotics not only generates short-chain fatty acids that acidify the intestinal environment and inhibit pathogen survival but also facilitates colonization of the intestinal mucosa and nutrient exchange within the gut microbiota [33,34]. Given the promising potential of amino acid-derived CDs in mitigating IBD, further exploration of the impact of CDs on redox balance and gut microbiota in conjunction with probiotics is imperative for future applications.

In this study, We used citric acid and 16 different amino acids as precursors to synthesize carbon dots with similar structures through one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Among the synthesized carbon dots, we selected the ones derived from histidine (His-CDs) as representative nanomedicines. This selection was based on their exceptional ability to scavenge free radicals and exhibit SOD enzyme-like properties. We also found that the surface groups of His-CDs, especially imidazole groups, played an important role in their SOD enzymatic activity. Furthermore, the bandgap of His-CDs was analyzed through UPS and UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra to investigate their catalytic reaction pathways. In vitro experiments demonstrated the remarkable antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of His-CDs. Notably, His-CDs were found to modulate gut microbiota by increasing the abundance of the Bacteroidetes phylum, while inhibiting Proteobacteria. This modulation led to the restoration of gut homeostasis. Fecal microbiota transplantation experiments confirmed that the administration of His-CDs up-regulated the abundance of beneficial bacteria, such as Muribaculaceae and Dubosiella, in the intestinal tract. Moreover, His-CDs effectively down-regulated the expression of pro-inflammatory factors and the content of ROS. They also promoted the restoration of damaged epithelial cells, thus alleviating colonic inflammation. Considering the potential interaction between gut microbiota and nanomedicine, we further validated using FMT that the attenuation of colitis by His-CDs may be mediated by regulating gut microbiota, leading to a reduction in inflammation and oxidative stress. Overall, this non-toxic His-CDs nanozyme shows promise as a therapeutic nanomedicine for IBD. It has the potential to achieve inflammation relief through a multipronged intervention strategy (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of histidine-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: His-CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method with histidine and citric acid as precursors. Fig. 1 a presented a representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, demonstrating the well-dispersed His-CDs with a quasi-spherical morphology and an average diameter of 2.5±0.4 nm (Fig. 1b). Further analysis using high-resolution TEM revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal face of graphite (inset, Fig. 1a) [35–37]. The X-ray diffraction pattern (Fig. 1c) exhibited a distinct peak at 2θ = 23°, which confirmed the presence of the (002) layer spacing and the graphite-like structure of His-CDs [35,38]. To analyze the structure and composition of the His-CDs, additional XPS and FT-IR measurements were performed.

The XPS survey spectrum of His-CDs (Fig. 1d) revealed three characteristic peaks corresponding to O 1s (10.05 At%), N 1s (17.74 At%), and C 1s (73.31 At%) at 532, 400, and 286 eV, respectively. For further analysis, Fig. 1e, 1f and S1a presented high-resolution (HR) XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. In the HR-C 1s spectrum of His-CDs, the peaks at 284.5, 285.8, and 287.7 eV were attributed to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, and C=O C bonds, respectively [39]. The HR-N 1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 399.5 and 400.7 eV, indicating the presence of nitrogen-containing heterocycles (such as pyrrole and pyridine) in the form of N=N and C=N bonds [40]. These findings suggested the presence of imidazolyl residue, which may contribute to the enzyme-like catalytic properties of His-CDs. In the HR-O 1s spectrum, two peaks at 531.6 and 532.8 eV were observed, representing C=O and O=H bonds, respectively [19,41]. The surface structure of His-CDs was further confirmed using FT-IR spectra (Fig. S1b). The peaks ranging from 3000∼3500 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of O-H, N-H, and C-H [42,43]. Additionally, the strong bands at 1614 and 1451 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C=C/C=O, while the absorption peak at 1231 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of C-O [44]. The characteristic absorption peak between 1350 and 1270 cm−1 was associated with the presence of C-N bonds [45]. Overall, these results confirmed the presence of various functional groups, including –OH, –NH2, C=C, and –COOH, on the surface of His-CDs. The presence of hydrophilic groups on His-CDs contributed to their excellent in vivo stability and biocompatibility.

To investigate the surface groups of His-CDs, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) and electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) were employed. The 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S2a) revealed that the peaks at 6.75 ppm and 7.55 ppm corresponded to the ketene and phenyl protons in histidine (designated as H1 and H2, respectively). Furthermore, the NMR peaks at 3.75 ppm and 4.7 ppm (H3 and H4, respectively) within the 3–5 ppm range were assigned to the protons near the electronegative O and N atoms. This finding was further supported by the ESI-MS results (Fig. S2b), where the dominant peaks at m/z 570.2 and 613.2 represented cyclic amide structures. Additionally, the peaks at m/z 613.2 and 657.26 indicated the presence of carboxyl groups on histidine. The peaks at m/z 789.9 and 790.3, corresponding to the loss of a hydrogen proton, indirectly confirmed the existence of imidazolyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging activities and multienzyme-like activities of His-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 16 amino acid-derived carbon dots were prepared under the same synthetic conditions. Among them, histidine-derived CDs (His-CDs) exhibited the most significant antioxidant activity compared to other amino acid-derived carbon dots (Fig. S3). Therefore, the scavenging activity of His-CDs on three representative free radicals was thoroughly investigated according to the literature precedent [20]. Through the 2,2′-diphenyl-1-pyridinium hydrazine (DPPH) free radical scavenging assay, it was found that His-CDs exhibited the highest antioxidant activity. As the concentration of His-CDs increased, the purple color in the mixed solution gradually diminished (Fig. 2 a). Importantly, when compared to histidine and citric acid, His-CDs demonstrated significantly superior efficacy in scavenging DPPH free radicals at equivalent concentrations. It is noteworthy that this high DPPH clearance rate of His-CDs is comparable to that of the other CDs (about 90%) on the basis of 500 μg/mL [18]. The ESR signal disappeared after the addition of His-CDs solution, which also indicated that His-CDs showed good antioxidant capacity (Fig. S4a). Furthermore, the scavenging activity of His-CDs remained unaffected despite significant fluctuations in the pH value of the mixed solution, ranging from 0.5 to 9 (Fig. S4b). This exceptional pH stability of His-CDs rendered them effective in challenging environments characterized by strong acids and alkalis, establishing a solid foundation for their outstanding antioxidant efficacy both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, Fig. 2b illustrated the ABTS free radical (ABTS+) scavenging effect of His-CDs. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, His-CDs exhibited a clearance rate for ABTS+ exceeding 90%, which is higher that of the other CDs (60%) at the same concentration [21]The scavenging ability of •OH in the Fenton reaction was evaluated using the H2O2/Fe2+ system. The characteristic absorption peak of •OH radical in the system gradually decreased as the concentration of His-CDs increased (Fig. S4c). Calculations indicated that His-CDs exhibited approximately 17% scavenging ability for •OH at a concentration of 500 μg/mL. The concentration-dependent SOD-like activity of His-CDs was investigated using 2-(2-methoxy-4-nitrophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2, 4-disulfonobenzene)–2H-tetrazolium monosodium salt (WST-8) as a color indicator. The presence of His-CDs led to a significant reduction in the formation of formazan, indicating their SOD-like activity, which was calculated to be 125 U/mg (Fig. 2c). Through comparing the SOD-like enzymatic activity of some nanozymes previously reported, we found that His-CDs is comparable with that of some nanozymes (Table S1). Additionally, considering the hazardous nature of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as a biologically relevant ROS [46], the CAT-like property of His-CDs was also studied. The results demonstrated that His-CDs facilitated the decomposition of H2O2 and exhibited concentration-dependent CAT-like enzyme activity (Fig. S4d). At a concentration of 500 μg/mL, the CAT-like enzyme activity of His-CDs was approximately 60 U/mg, significantly higher than that of the precursor at the same concentration. Therefore, His-CDs demonstrate SOD and CAT nanozyme activities, making them potential candidates for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory applications in vitro and in vivo research

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deciphering the mechanism of antioxidant and enzyme-like activities of His-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with antioxidant and SOD like activity can scavenge free radicals, thereby exhibiting a relieving effect on diseases caused by oxidative stress. It should be noted that the catalytic activity and specificity of CDs are closely related to its structure [20,22]. Therefore, it is of great significance to clarify the structure–activity relationship. Firstly, ascorbic acid (with a higher reducing ability than citric acid) and glucose (with a lower reducing ability than citric acid) were used as substitute precursors to investigate the impact of precursor reducibility on the antioxidant activity of CDs. The CDs derived from ascorbic acid and glucose exhibited weaker free radical scavenging activity compared to His-CDs, and no consistent change related to the degree of reducibility was observed. This suggests that the antioxidant capability of His-CDs is not affected by the reducibility of the precursors (Fig. S5a and S5b). And then, leucine, which does not contain imidazole groups, was used as a precursor to assess the effect of imidazole groups on the properties of His-CDs. The results presented in Fig. S5c and S5d showed a significant decrease in the antioxidant activity of CDs derived from leucine (Leu-CDs). Based on the results obtained from XPS and FT-IR, it was observed that the surface of His-CDs contained imidazole residues. Therefore, the antioxidant activity of His-CDs is closely related to the imidazole groups on their surface.

Imidazole is known to be a strong nucleophilic group and a generalized acid-base functional group, which has a significant impact on the enzyme-like functions of His-CDs [47]. Moreover, oxygen-containing groups of His-CDs, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, can serve as SOD like active sites [22]. The conjugated double bonds can facilitate electron transfer and stabilize intermediates containing unpaired electrons, thereby jointly promoting free radical addition reactions [48]. Further confirm the catalytic activity of His-CDs by calculating their band structures. The Eg of His-CDs was 1.95 eV by measuring the x-axis intercept of an extrapolated line from the linear regime of the Tauc plot (Fig. S6a) [49]. The valence band energy (Ev ) was calculated to be 6.97 eV from UPS (Fig. S6b), and subsequently, the conduction band energy (Ec ) was estimated as 5.02 eV by subtracting Eg from Ev , converting into electrochemical energy potential in volts (V) were 2.52 V and 0.58 V, respectively. Considering that the standard redox potentials of O2/H2O2 (0.695 V), O2/O2•− (0.73 V), O2•−/H2O2 (1.5 V), and H2O2/H2O (1.776 V) all fall within the band range of His-CDs [50], it can be inferred that the catalytic reaction mechanism of His-CDs as follows:
(1)

Additionally, His-CDs also can store and release electrons [51], ensuring the continuous advancement of the reaction. Overall, the analysis results of the antioxidant and enzyme-like activities mechanism further demonstrate that His-CDs are a material worth further exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: His-CDs protected cells/nematodes from oxidative damage in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidant and multienzyme-like activities are key factors for the biological application of nanomaterials [11–15]. Therefore, the therapeutic effects of His-CDs in vitro and in vivo were explored. For in vitro cytotoxicity, as illustrated in Fig. S7a, His-CDs exhibited minimal cytotoxicity, with cell activity maintained at approximately 80% even at a concentration of 0.625 mg/mL. Furthermore, the ability of small-sized His-CDs to be taken up by RAW264.7 cells in 1h and effectively maintain for 24h was observed, as indicated by Fig. S7b. The safety of His-CDs at the nematode level was further confirmed by evaluating the body length and running times of the nematodes. The results presented in Fig. S7c to S7e demonstrated that feeding nematodes with various concentrations of His-CDs did not significantly affect their growth, body length, or running times, as compared to the OP50 group. The above results indicate that His-CDs possess good biocompatibility. Moreover, nematodes fed with the mixed food (containing His-CDs) exhibited a noticeable increase in the content of total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC) compared to those fed with OP50 alone (control group), thus suggesting a positive effect on the antioxidant capacity of the nematodes (Fig. S7f).

Given the significant free radical scavenging activity and good biosafety of His-CDs, our subsequent investigation aimed to evaluate their potential in mitigating oxidative stress within a cellular environment. To assess the ability of His-CDs to alleviate the oxidative damage induced by exogenous H2O2 (at a concentration of 1 mM) on normal colonic epithelial cells (NCM 460 cells), we performed live and dead cell staining using Calcein acetyl methyl ester (Calcein) and propyl iodide (PI). Calcein stains living cells in green, while PI identifies dead cells in red. In Fig. S8, it was evident that the co-treatment of His-CDs and H2O2 resulted in a gradual reduction in dead cells compared to H2O2 treatment alone. The results of the apoptosis experiment (Fig. 2d) further supported this finding. In the control group, NCM 460 cells did not undergo significant apoptosis. However, in the H2O2 treatment group, both early and late apoptotic cells accounted for 32.4% and 9.9%, respectively. Following treatment with different concentrations of His-CDs, the early and late apoptosis rates of cells were decreased, accounting for 2.82% and 11.7% at 100 μg/mL, respectively.

Additionally, to induce excess production of free radicals in both RAW 264.7 cells and nematodes, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and juglone were utilized as stimulants, respectively. Upon intervention with His-CDs, the level of intracellular ROS demonstrated a significant decrease, with essentially complete clearance observed at a concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 2e, 2f, and S9a). The quantity of malondialdehyde (MDA) serves as an indicator of bodily peroxidation and indirectly reflects the extent of cellular damage. As depicted in Fig. 2g, His-CDs intervention effectively mitigated the oxidative damage caused by juglone in nematodes. Similar outcomes were observed at the mRNA level, as His-CDs intervention upregulated the mRNA expression of oxidative stress-related genes such as SOD-1 and GST-1 in C. elegans (Fig. 2h and 2i).

We further investigated whether the antioxidant capacity of His-CDs also manifests as an anti-inflammatory effect. Fig. S9b depicts the content of the inflammatory cytokine IL-1β in the cell culture supernatant of different treatment groups after His-CDs intervention. Furthermore, IL-1β levels were significantly up-regulated in the LPS model group, whereas IL-1β levels were significantly reduced in the His-CDs intervention group. Similar results were observed regarding the mRNA levels of inflammatory cytokines. The His-CDs intervention group exhibited significant down-regulation of IL-1β and TNF-α mRNA levels, respectively, compared to the LPS group, as shown in Figs. S9c and S9d. All the aforementioned experimental results demonstrated that His-CDs reduced intracellular ROS levels in a dose-dependent manner, thereby protecting cells/nematodes from oxidative damage. Additionally, His-CDs were capable of regulating cytokine levels, resulting in an anti-inflammatory effect at both the cellular and nematode levels. These findings provide a potential application for His-CDs in in vivo anti-inflammatory therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: His-CDs relieved acute colitis induced by DSS
ce:section.ce:para: Male C57BL/6 mice (mean weight, 20 ± 2 g; Age, 7 weeks) were kept in a 12-hour light cycle and had ad libitum access to food and water. There were six mice in each group. After acclimation for 1 week, the mice were randomly divided into control group, glucan sulfate-induced colitis model group (DSS group), low concentration CDs group (CDs-L, 2.5 mg/kg), medium concentration CDs group (CDs-M, 12.5 mg/kg), and high concentration CDs group (CDs-H, 25 mg/kg) (Fig. 3 a). Except for the control group, mice in each group were orally administered with 200 μL of sterile saline and different doses of CDs from day 8 to day 21. Additionally, the colitis model was induced in mice by administering 2.5% (w/v) DSS from day 0 to day 7. As shown in Fig. 3b, the administration of CDs mitigated weight loss in mice with colitis. After 7 days of modeling, the DAI scores of the DSS group and CDs groups at different concentrations reached approximately 8. After 14 days of oral administration, the DAI score in the DSS group remained around 8, indicating that oral administration of sterile water did not reduce the DAI score. Conversely, the DAI scores of mice in the CDs-L, CDs-M, and CDs-H groups showed a decreasing trend (Fig. 3c) [52]. Statistical analysis demonstrated that the administration of low, medium, and high concentrations of CDs alleviated colonic shortening in mice with colitis (Fig. 3d), a result supported by the representative colon photograph in Fig. 3e and Fig. S10. Histopathological scoring was performed based on the H&E staining of colon tissue from mice in each group. In the DSS group, colon tissue exhibited goblet cell deletion, inflammatory cell infiltration, and mucosal injury. In contrast, the colonic tissue of mice treated with low, medium, and high concentrations of CDs remained almost intact, with significantly lower histopathological scores compared to the model group (Fig. 3f and S11). All the aforementioned results indicated that CD intervention attenuated DSS-induced colitis in mice and reduced intestinal mucous layer damage. Unfortunately, although the results of Fig. S5e and f show that the free radical scavenging activity of 300–500 μg/mL His-CDs are slightly higher than that of 5-ASA, respectively, the practical application value of His-CDs cannot be evaluated due to the lack of a positive drug control group in animal experiments.

After confirming the therapeutic effect, we proceeded to investigate the potential mechanisms underlying the alleviation of DSS-induced acute colitis by His-CDs. Considering the crucial role of REDOX in maintaining intestinal inflammation homeostasis, we examined the changes in REDOX-related markers in the colon tissues of mice. As depicted in Fig. 3g and h, treatment with His-CDs resulted in increased activity of SOD and GSH, both of which are vital components of the body's antioxidant defense system. Concurrently, His-CDs treatment significantly reduced the level of MDA, a marker of peroxidation, in the colon tissue of mice with acute lipid colitis (Fig. 3i). This reduction indirectly suggested that His-CDs treatment mitigated cell damage. MPO is expressed by neutrophils and catalyzes the production of active substances and hypochlorous acid in inflammatory tissues, thereby participating in the regulation of IBD [53]. In the DSS group, the level of MPO in colon tissues was significantly increased, while its accumulation level was markedly decreased after His-CDs treatment, confirming the successful improvement of IBD symptoms (Fig. 3j). Altogether, these findings indicate that His-CDs exert a regulatory influence on REDOX balance, with a stronger effect observed at higher concentrations. His-CDs exhibit the ability to regulate the REDOX balance in mice with colitis, as well as effectively restore damaged epithelial cells, while significantly decreasing the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, thus alleviating colonic inflammation. His-CDs intervention led to the down-regulation of mRNA relative expression of pro-inflammatory factors IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-18, while up-regulating the mRNA relative expression of anti-inflammatory factor IL-10 (Fig. 3k-n).

Once the therapeutic effect of His-CDs was confirmed, our investigation extended to explore additional potential mechanisms underlying the alleviation of DSS-induced acute colitis. The intestinal mucosal mechanical barrier, comprising tight junctions (TJ), serves as the primary defense against foreign invasion. TJ represents a complex junction composed of various proteins and molecules and is widely distributed among neighboring intestinal epithelial cells. Among these proteins are three transmembrane proteins: claudin, occludin, and ZO-1 [12]. Excessive production of ROS can damage the protective mucus layer, resulting in increased exposure of epithelial cells to pathogens and environmental toxins within IBD lesions. Consequently, this leads to loss of intestinal permeability and compromised structural integrity [11]. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed a significant decrease in the expression of ZO-1, a crucial TJ protein, in the colon tissue of the DSS group compared to the control group. Following His-CDs intervention, except for the low-dose group, the expression level of ZO-1 in the colon tissues of mice was significantly higher than that in the model group, particularly in the medium and high-concentration His-CDs intervention groups (Fig. 4 a and b). Alcian blue staining demonstrated that the mucin area and goblet cell count were significantly reduced in colitis mice compared to control mice. Nonetheless, mice treated with different concentrations of His-CDs exhibited a substantial increase in mucin area and goblet cell number (Fig. 4c and d). Analysis of mRNA expression using RT-qPCR revealed a significant decrease in the mRNA expression of TJ proteins, including occludin, Claudin-1, and ZO-1, in the model group compared to the control group. Following His-CDs intervention, the mRNA expression levels of occludin, Claudin-1, and ZO-1 in mouse colon tissue were significantly upregulated (Fig. 4e-g). Western Blot analysis further confirmed a significant decrease in the expression of tight junction protein occludin in the model group compared to the control group (Fig. 4h and Fig. S12a). Collectively, these findings suggested that His-CDs mitigated damage to the intestinal epithelial barrier by upregulating the expression of TJ-related proteins (Fig. S12b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: His-CDs mitigated DSS-induced intestinal microbiota changes
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction of fecal microorganisms' genome and subsequent bioinformatics analysis were conducted as described previously [54]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the control, DSS, and His-CDs groups comprised 114, 75, and 99 distinct microbial species, respectively. Notably, the number of OTUs in the DSS group was found to be lower compared to both the control and His-CDs groups. This observation indicated that His-CDs intervention alleviated the decline in OTU count induced by DSS while enhancing the diversity of gut microbiota in mice. To explore the impact of His-CDs on gut microbiota structure, principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) and cluster tree analysis based on β diversity were performed. The distance between the control and DSS groups indicated a notable deviation in the intestinal microflora structure of the DSS group compared to the control group, while the His-CDs group exhibited greater similarity (Fig. 5b). The results of the cluster tree analysis were consistent with those obtained from PCoA (Fig. S13). The phylogenetic tree integrated the intestinal microbiome of each group into two main branches: the DSS group and the control group (which included the control group and the CDs group). Furthermore, the His-CDs group demonstrated significantly higher community diversity (Simpson index) and community richness (Sobs) when compared to the DSS group (Fig. 5c-e). Consequently, His-CDs intervention mitigated the decrease in gut microbiota diversity and richness caused by DSS exposure.

At the phylum level, Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes were found to be the dominant phyla in the fecal microbiota (Fig. 5f). Previous studies have suggested that the ratio of Firmicutes to Bacteroidetes is positively correlated with the inflammation level in the body [55]. In comparison to the control group, the gut of the DSS group exhibited a significant increase in the relative abundance of Firmicutes and a significant decrease in the relative abundance of Bacteroidetes. On the other hand, the His-CDs group displayed a significant increase in the relative abundance of Bacteroidetes and a significant decrease in the relative abundances of Desulphurobacteria and Proteobacteria, when compared to the mice in the DSS group. At the genus level, the His-CDs group showed a significant increase in the relative abundances of norank_f_Muribaculaceae, Dubosiella, and Allobaculum in the intestinal tract (Fig. 5g-i). These bacteria have been demonstrated to positively contribute to the enhancement of intestinal epithelial homeostasis and the mitigation of IBD [56,57]. Studies have shown that norank_f_Muribaculaceae can reduce inflammation, inhibit harmful bacteria and oxidative stress, and improve intestinal mucosal inflammation [58]. Dubosiella can regulate the immune balance of Treg/Th17 in the colon by producing short-chain fatty acids and lysine, thereby inhibiting colonic inflammatory response and improving mucosal barrier damage [59]. Allobaculum have a physiological ability to produce short-chain fatty acids from carbohydrates, which has a good effect on lowering blood sugar [60]. In addition, studies have shown that Turicibacter is associated with the development of colorectal cancer, with potential mechanisms including promotion of chronic inflammation, DNA damage, and production of bioactive carcinogenic metabolites [61]. In this study, we found that His-CDs treatment also significantly reduced the abundance of Turicibacter in DSS induced colitis (Fig. 5g). Thus, His-CDs intervention can alter the composition of gut microbiota in colitis mice, reduce the abundance of harmful bacteria, and increase the abundance of beneficial bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-colitis effect of His-CDs can be transferred by fecal microbiota transplantation
ce:section.ce:para: The sequencing results of 16S rDNA have demonstrated that His-CDs can restore DSS-induced intestinal microecological disorders in mice. Therefore, we further conducted the fecal microbiota transplantation experiment (FMT) to verify whether these upregulated microorganisms (norank_f_Muribaculaceae, Dubosiella, and Allobaculum, etc.) play a positive role in the antagonistic process of colitis (Fig. 6a). As depicted in Fig. 6 b, both the FMT-CDs and FMT-DSS groups exhibited lower weight recovery rates compared to the FMT-Con group. Notably, during the initial 6 days of FMT administration, the mice in the FMT-DSS and FMT-CDs groups displayed similar weight changes. However, on the 7th day of gavage, significant weight loss and fluctuations in weight change were observed in the FMT-DSS group. After 7 days of modeling, the DAI scores of the FMT-DSS and FMT-CDs groups reached approximately 8. After 7 days of FMT administration, the DAI score in the DSS group remained around 8, indicating that oral administration of sterile water did not reduce the DAI score. Conversely, the DAI scores of mice in the FMT-CDs group showed a decreasing trend (Fig. 6c). Furthermore, the FMT-DSS group exhibited a significantly shorter colon length compared to the FMT-CDs group (Fig. 6d, 6e and S14a). Compared with the FMT-Con, goblet cell deletion and mucosal injury were observed in the FMT-DSS group. In contrast, the colon tissue of mice in the FMT-CDs group appeared more intact (Fig. 6f).Correspondingly, the histopathological scores of colitis mice in FMT-CDs group were also significantly reduced after intragastric administration of stool bacteria suspension (Fig. S14b). Thus, the microbes in the stool of the His-CDs group mitigated the histopathological damage induced by DSS. ELISA and qPCR results also proved that fecal bacterial suspension in His-CDs group could alleviate the overexpression of proinflammatory factors in colitis mice. As shown in Fig. 6g, IL-1β content in colon tissue of mice in FMT-CDs group was significantly down-regulated. Various pro-inflammatory cytokines also showed the same trend at the mRNA level (Fig. 6h-j). Compared with mice in FMT-DSS group, mRNA levels of IL-1β, TNF-α and IL-18 in FMT-CDs group were also significantly down-regulated. All the above results indicate that His-CDs can affect the composition of intestinal microenvironment. At the same time, these altered microbiomes can also effectively alleviate DSS induced colitis by regulating the production of inflammatory factors.

The experimental results of FMT have proved that mouse faecal bacterial suspension after His-CDs intervention can indeed alleviate DSS induced colitis in mice. To further determine the presence of these beneficial bacteria (norank_f_Muribaculaceae, Dubosiella, and Allobaculum, etc.), we collected the stool of each group of mice after FMT treatment for 16S rDNA sequencing. As shown in Fig. S15a, the OTUs of fecal microorganisms in FMT-CDs group (492) was higher than that in FMT-Con group (446) and FMT-DSS group (456). The results of principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) showed that the structure of intestinal flora in FMT-CDs and FMT-Con groups was more similar than that in FMT-DSS group (Fig. S15b). In addition, the diversity and richness of gut microbiota in FMT-CDs group were significantly higher than those in FMT-DSS group (Figs. S16a-c). Notably, in comparison to the FMT-DSS group, the FMT-CDs group exhibited a significant increase in the relative abundance of Bacteroidetes, while the relative abundances of Verrucomicrobia and Actinobacteriota significantly decreased (Fig. S16d). At the genus level, there was also a significant increase in the relative abundance of Dubosiella in the fecal microbes of mice in the FMT-CDs group, which has also recently been shown to alleviate IBD (Fig. S16e) [62]. Therefore, His-CDs can increase the abundance of beneficial intestinal bacteria, and through the synergistic anti-inflammatory effect of probiotics, it can be further applied in the treatment of IBD.

The gut microenvironment consists of a large number of specific proportions of microbiota and their metabolites, which play a crucial role in protecting the intestinal mucosa, absorbing and transferring energy, and metabolizing nutrients. The gut microbiota, as the “eighth human organ”, represents a dynamic interaction determined jointly by host, microbial self-selection, and environmental factors. This organic alliance is particularly important in host immune defense, metabolic nutrient absorption, and health maintenance and disease remission processes. Our experimental results of FMT showed that His-CDs could indeed mediate intestinal flora homeostasis to alleviate IBD. This strategy of regulating gut microbiota not only broadens the application range of CDs, but also provides a new way of thinking for the treatment of IBD.

It is well known that materials with good blood biocompatibility are the basis for in vivo applications. Therefore, we evaluated the blood biocompatibility of His-CDs by hemolysis test. As shown in the Fig. 7 a and b, His-CDs exhibitd excellent blood compatibility. The structure of the red blood cells remained intact, further confirming their good biocompatibility. Moreover, the biosafety of His-CDs was assessed through a health control study. Male C57BL/6 mice were administered a daily dose of 25 mg/kg for 14 days. Throughout the treatment period, His-CDs did not elicit any significant abnormalities in the mice's behavior, and there were no notable weight changes observed between the His-CDs and PBS-treated mice. One day after the final treatment, the mice were euthanized, and major organs such as the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys were surgically extracted for histological analysis. Histological sections stained with H&E and treated with His-CDs exhibited no apparent signs of histological damage (Fig. 7c). These findings indicated that His-CDs demonstrated a favorable biosafety profile when administered under the current treatment conditions.

His-CDs exhibited exceptional scavenging capabilities against ROS and possessed potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Our initial findings indicated that the surface groups of His-CDs, particularly the imidazole groups, played a vital role in the enzymatic activity of SOD. In addition, we used UPS and UV–vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy to calculate the band gap of His-CDs and preliminarily analyzed the catalytic reaction pathways. In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that His-CDs effectively mitigated oxidative stress damage in cells and animals (including nematodes and mice) by modulating redox balance, thus offering a treatment for colitis. Remarkably, 16S rDNA and fecal microbiota transplantation experiments revealed that His-CDs regulated the composition and structure of the gut microbiota, effectively alleviating DSS-induced colitis in mice. Furthermore, the enhancement of mucosal adhesion and modulation of the immune response contributed to the therapeutic effect of His-CDs on colitis. Overall, this non-toxic CD nanozyme held tremendous promise as a therapeutic nanomedicine for IBD, providing multiple intervention strategies for relieving inflammation.
